title: Setup & Mental Model
description: Install MongoDB, understand the document model, and map your SQL knowledge to MongoDB concepts.

sections.content: MongoDB is a **document database** — instead of storing data in rows and columns like PostgreSQL, it stores data as JSON-like **documents** inside **collections**. Think of a document as a very flexible row that can hold nested objects, arrays, and mixed types all in one place.

sections.heading: A PostgreSQL row vs. a MongoDB document

sections.content: **Key insight:** In PostgreSQL you normalise everything into separate tables and JOINs bring them back together. In MongoDB, related data is often stored together inside the same document — fewer reads, simpler queries.
You already have MongoDB running locally (confirmed during setup). Here are the three tools you'll use throughout this course:

sections.heading: Your MongoDB toolkit
sections.content:
# mongosh — the MongoDB shell (like psql for PostgreSQL)
mongosh

# Common mongosh commands
show dbs           # list databases  →  \l in psql
use bookstore      # switch database →  \c bookstore in psql
show collections   # list tables     →  \dt in psql
db.books.find()    # query a table   →  SELECT * FROM books;

# Exit the shell
exit

**MongoDB Compass** is a GUI tool (like pgAdmin or TablePlus) — great for browsing documents visually. Download it from mongodb.com/try/download/compass. This course also has a built-in playground tab you can use directly in the browser.
Every document in MongoDB is stored in **BSON** (Binary JSON), which gives you richer types than plain JSON. Here are all the types you'll encounter:

sections.heading: BSON types with SQL equivalents
sections.content:
{
  _id:          ObjectId("..."),    // Auto-generated unique ID (like SERIAL PRIMARY KEY)
  title:        "1984",             // String   → VARCHAR / TEXT
  publishedYear: 1949,              // Integer  → INT
  price:        12.99,              // Double   → DECIMAL
  inStock:      true,               // Boolean  → BOOLEAN
  tags:         ["classic", "dystopia"],  // Array  → no SQL equivalent (or junction table)
  author: {                         // Embedded document → no SQL equiv (or FK to another table)
    name: "Orwell",
    born: 1903
  },
  publishedAt:  ISODate("1949-06-08"),  // Date  → TIMESTAMP
  deletedAt:    null                    // Null  → NULL
}
**Watch out:** MongoDB field names are case-sensitive. `publishedYear` and `PublishedYear` are different fields. PostgreSQL columns are case-insensitive by default.

**The _id field:** MongoDB automatically creates `_id` as an ObjectId (a 12-byte unique identifier) unless you provide your own. It's indexed automatically — just like a PRIMARY KEY. You can use any unique value (string, int, UUID) as `_id` if you prefer.

**ObjectId is not just a random number.** It is a 12-byte value that encodes four pieces of information: a 4-byte Unix timestamp (seconds since epoch), a 5-byte random value unique to the machine and process, and a 3-byte incrementing counter. This design guarantees uniqueness across many servers inserting at the same time — no central coordinator needed.

**Providing your own _id:** You can set `_id` to any unique value — a string, integer, or UUID — by including it in the document: `db.books.insertOne({ _id: '978-0451524935', title: '1984' })`. MongoDB uses your value instead of generating an ObjectId.

**The 16 MB document size limit:** A single MongoDB document cannot exceed 16 MB. This is usually plenty, but it explains why you should never embed a list that could grow very large (like all orders for a user, or all comments on a post). Those belong in a separate collection.

**Lazy creation:** MongoDB does not require you to run `CREATE DATABASE` or `CREATE TABLE`. Databases and collections are created automatically the first time you insert a document. Running `use newdb` in mongosh just sets the active database — it does not create anything. The first `insertOne()` does.

lessons.title: MongoDB in Distributed Systems: ACID, BASE & Replica Sets

sections.content: PostgreSQL gives you full ACID guarantees on every transaction. MongoDB's story is more nuanced — it depends on the scope of the operation.

**Single-document operations are always ACID.** When you call `insertOne`, `updateOne`, or `deleteOne`, that operation is atomic, consistent, isolated, and durable. Either the full write succeeds or nothing changes — no partial states.

**Multi-document operations are NOT atomic by default.** If you call `updateMany` on 1000 documents and the server crashes halfway, some documents will be updated and some won't. For operations that must succeed or fail as a unit across multiple documents (like a bank transfer), you need an explicit **transaction** (available since MongoDB 4.0).

sections.heading: Multi-document transaction (MongoDB 4.0+)

sections.content: **BASE vs ACID** — In distributed databases the trade-off has a name: BASE stands for **Basically Available** (the system stays responsive even under failures), **Soft state** (data may temporarily differ across nodes), and **Eventual consistency** (all nodes will eventually agree on the same data). This contrasts with ACID's strict consistency guarantees.

**Replica Sets — high availability.** A replica set is a group of MongoDB servers that all hold the same data. There is one **primary** node (handles all writes) and one or more **secondary** nodes (replicate data from the primary and can serve reads). If the primary goes down, the remaining members hold an automatic **election** and one of the secondaries becomes the new primary — typically within 10–30 seconds. Your application reconnects automatically.

sections.heading: Replica set structure
sections.content:
Primary  ←──── client writes go here
   │
   ├── Secondary 1  (replicates from primary, can serve reads)
   └── Secondary 2  (replicates from primary, can serve reads)

If primary fails:
  → Secondaries hold an election
  → One secondary is promoted to primary
  → Application reconnects automatically

**Write Concern** controls how durable a write is before MongoDB confirms it. `w: 1` (default) means the primary acknowledges the write. `w: 'majority'` means more than half the replica set must confirm before MongoDB responds — safer but slightly slower.

**Capped Collections** are fixed-size collections that work like circular buffers. When a capped collection reaches its size limit, the oldest documents are automatically overwritten by new ones. Useful for logs and event streams where you only care about the most recent N entries.

sections.heading: Creating a capped collection
